Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 93 BY SENATORS MIZELL, ABRAHAM, BARROW, HODGES AND MORRIS 1 AN ACT 2 To amend and reenact Code of Criminal Procedure Arts. 312(A), (B), the introductory 3 paragraph of (D), and (1) and (2), and (G)(2)(a), 316(1), (9), and (10), 318, 319(A), 4 and 320(J)(1) and to enact Code of Criminal Procedure Arts. 312(G)(2)(c) and (d), 5 312.1, 315(D), and 316(11) through (13), relative to bail; to provide relative to bail 6 before and after conviction; to provide relative to revocation of bail in certain 7 circumstances; to provide for definitions; to provide relative to schedules of bail; to 8 provide for minimum amounts of bail for certain offenses; to provide for factors in 9 fixing bail; to provide relative to juvenile records in fixing bail; to provide for bail 10 conditions for crimes of violence; and to provide for related matters. 11 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 12 Section 1. Code of Criminal Procedure Arts. 312(A), (B), the introductory paragraph 13 of (D), and (1) and (2), and (G)(2)(a), 316(1), (9), and (10), 318, 319(A), and 320(J)(1) are 14 hereby amended and reenacted and Code of Criminal Procedure Arts. 312(G)(2)(c) and (d), 15 312.1, 315(D), and 316(11) through (13) are hereby enacted to read as follows: 16 Art. 312. Right to bail before and after conviction 17 A. Except as provided in this Article and, Article 312.1, Article 313, or 18 Article 313.1, a person in custody who is charged with the commission of an offense 19 is entitled to bail before conviction. 20 B.(1) A person released on a previously posted bail undertaking for any of 21 the following shall not be readmitted to bail when the person previously failed 22 to appear and a warrant for arrest was issued and not recalled or the previous 23 bail undertaking has been revoked or forfeited: 24 (1) a (a) A crime of violence as defined by R.S. 14:2(B) which carries a 25 minimum mandatory sentence of imprisonment upon conviction or. 26 (2) the (b) The production, manufacture, distribution, or dispensing or 27 possession with intent to produce, manufacture, distribute, or dispense a controlled 28 dangerous substance as defined by the Louisiana Uniform Controlled Dangerous SB NO. 93 ENROLLED 1 Substances Law, shall not be readmitted to bail when the person previously failed to 2 appear and a warrant for arrest was issued and not recalled or the previous bail 3 undertaking has been revoked or forfeited. 4 (c) Any offense involving the possession or use of a firearm. 5 (d) Any sex offense as defined by R.S. 15:541. 6 (2) If a person voluntarily appears without confinement by a law enforcement 7 officer or bail recovery agent following a motion to revoke bail or issuance of an 8 arrest warrant for failure to appear but prior to revocation or forfeiture, then he may 9 be released only under one of the following circumstances: 10 (1)(a) After a contradictory hearing, a person may be released on the 11 previously posted bail undertaking if the motion to revoke bail is rescinded or the 12 arrest warrant is recalled and the surety is present or represented at the hearing and 13 gives written consent. Previous instances of revocation and forfeiture in unrelated 14 cases are admissible at the hearing. This relief is available only once. 15 (2)(b) A person may be released on a new bail undertaking without a 16 contradictory hearing only on bail with a commercial surety and in an amount higher 17 than the original bail. 18 * * * 19 D. A convicted person shall be remanded to jail to await sentence, and shall 20 be remanded to custody after sentencing, unless any of the following occur: 21 (1) He is allowed to remain free on a bail undertaking posted prior to 22 conviction by operation of Article 331(A), and the bail previously fixed is in 23 accordance with all of the applicable provisions of this Article and other applicable 24 law. 25 (2) He is released by virtue of a bail undertaking posted after conviction, and 26 the bail was fixed in accordance with this Article and other applicable law. 27 However, post conviction bail is not authorized pursuant to this Article when 28 there is proof of a substantial risk that the person may flee or poses an 29 imminent danger to any other person or the community, including when the 30 court finds the convicted person is likely to commit other crimes while on bail SB NO. 93 ENROLLED 1 and is therefore a danger to the community. 2 * * * 3 G.(1) * * * 4 (2)(a) After conviction of any crime punishable by imprisonment for twenty- 5 five years or more that is both either a sex offense and or a crime of violence, there 6 shall be a rebuttable presumption that the release of the person convicted will pose 7 a danger to another person or the community and that there is a substantial risk that 8 the person convicted might flee. 9 * * * 10 (c) This Paragraph applies based on the cumulative maximum sentences 11 for all crimes for which a person was convicted, provided that any crime for 12 which the person has been convicted is a sex offense or crime of violence as 13 defined in this Paragraph. 14 (d) This Paragraph also applies to any person who has been charged as 15 a habitual offender pursuant to R.S. 15:529.1. 16 * * * 17 Art. 312.1. Revocation of bail upon subsequent felony arrest 18 A. If a defendant is admitted to bail for any crime of violence, any sex 19 offense, or any crime involving the possession or use of a firearm, the bail for 20 such offense shall be revoked upon any subsequent arrest for a felony offense 21 that is not a part of the same transaction, occurrence, or criminal episode which 22 resulted in the arrest that is the basis for the defendant's current admittance to 23 bail. 24 B.(1) Prior to booking the defendant, the arresting agency shall conduct 25 a prompt and diligent search to determine if the defendant is currently on bail 26 for any crime of violence, any sex offense, or any crime involving the possession 27 or use of a firearm. 28 (2) The arresting agency shall provide the court with the information 29 described in Subparagraph (1) of this Paragraph, along with an updated 30 criminal history with all other booking information, for the court to consider SB NO. 93 ENROLLED 1 when determining the defendant's entitlement to bail, the amount of bail, and 2 conditions of bail. 3 C. If the court determines that the defendant is currently on bail for any 4 crime of violence, any sex offense, or any crime involving the possession or use 5 of a firearm, the bail for such offense shall be revoked upon any subsequent 6 arrest for a felony offense that is not a part of the same transaction, occurrence, 7 or criminal episode which resulted in the arrest that is the basis for the 8 defendant's current admittance to bail. The court shall enter an order revoking 9 such prior bail, pending a bail hearing which may be invoked by the state or the 10 defendant. Either party may seek supervisory review of the granting or denial 11 of any order that revokes the defendant's prior admittance to bail. 12 D. For the purposes of this Article, the following terms have the 13 following meanings: 14 (1) "Crime of violence" means any offense defined or enumerated as a 15 crime of violence in R.S. 14:2(B). 16 (2) "Sex offense" means any offense that requires registration and 17 notification pursuant to R.S. 15:540 et seq. 18 * * * 19 Art. 315. Schedules of bail 20 * * * 21 D. A bail schedule implemented by a district court shall fix the minimum 22 bail amount for the following offenses, and any court that does not utilize a bail 23 schedule shall not approve a bail undertaking that is less than the minimum 24 amount specified for the following offenses: 25 (1) For possession of child sexual abuse materials, R.S. 14:81.1, fifty 26 thousand dollars. 27 (2) For production of child sexual abuse materials, R.S. 14:81.1, one 28 hundred thousand dollars. 29 Art. 316. Factors in fixing amount of bail 30 The amount of bail shall be fixed in an amount that will ensure the presence SB NO. 93 ENROLLED 1 of the defendant, as required, and the safety of any other person and the community, 2 having regard to: 3 (1) The seriousness of the offense charged, including but not limited to 4 whether the offense is a crime of violence, sex offense, or involves a controlled 5 dangerous substance. 6 * * * 7 (9) Whether a firearm or other dangerous weapon was possessed or used 8 during the commission of the offense. 9 (10) Whether available resources exist within the jurisdiction to impose 10 enforceable conditions that would help ensure the defendant's appearance and 11 protect the community. 12 (11) The likelihood that the defendant, if released on bail, will continue 13 to commit crimes resulting in harm to any person or the property of another. 14 (9)(12) Any other circumstances affecting the probability of defendant's 15 appearance. 16 (10)(13) The type or form of bail. 17 * * * 18 Art. 318. Juvenile records in fixing bail 19 A. For the purpose of fixing bail, a court may make a written request of any 20 juvenile court for an abstract containing only the pending or adjudicated delinquent 21 acts of a defendant currently before the requesting court. The request shall be 22 promptly complied with; however, juvenile court shall promptly comply with the 23 request; however, not more than forty-eight hours, exclusive of Saturdays, Sundays, 24 and legal holidays, shall lapse before the requested information is deposited in the 25 mail, by means of an email address provided in the written request addressed to 26 the requesting court. 27 B. The requesting court shall not copy, duplicate, or otherwise reproduce 28 such juvenile records, and these shall be deposited in the mail and addressed to the 29 issuing juvenile court within seventy-two hours, exclusive of Saturdays, Sundays, 30 and legal holidays, after bail is determined issue a protective order concerning the SB NO. 93 ENROLLED 1 use of the juvenile abstract for the purposes of protecting the confidentiality of 2 the abstract from any individual who is not involved in the relevant proceedings 3 that concern the defendant who is currently before the requesting court. 4 Art. 319. Modifications of bail 5 A. The court having trial jurisdiction over the offense charged, on its own 6 motion or on motion of the prosecuting attorney or defendant, for good cause, may 7 either increase or reduce, subject to any minimum amount provided by law, the 8 amount of bail, or require new or additional security. For purposes of this Article, 9 good cause for increase of bail specifically includes but is not limited to the rearrest 10 of the defendant on offenses alleged to have been committed while out on a bail 11 undertaking. The modification of any bail order wherein a bail undertaking has been 12 posted by a criminal defendant and his sureties shall upon the modification terminate 13 the liability of the defendant and his sureties under the previously existing bail 14 undertaking. A new bail undertaking must be posted in the amount of the new bail 15 order. 16 * * * 17 Art. 320. Conditions of bail undertaking 18 * * * 19 J.(1) Crimes of violence. Notwithstanding the provisions of Paragraph G of 20 this Article and notwithstanding any other provision of law to the contrary, if the 21 defendant is alleged to have committed a crime of violence as defined in R.S. 22 14:2(B), the court shall require as a condition the following conditions of bail: 23 (a)(i) that That the defendant refrain from going to the residence or 24 household of the victim, the victim's school, and the victim's place of employment 25 or otherwise contacting the victim in any manner whatsoever, and shall refrain from 26 having any further contact with the victim. The court shall also require as a condition 27 of bail that the defendant be prohibited from communicating, by electronic 28 communication, in writing, or orally, with a victim of the offense, or with any of the 29 victim's immediate family members. 30 (ii) This condition does not apply if the victim consents by way of a request SB NO. 93 ENROLLED 1 to the court and the court issues an order permitting the communication. If an 2 immediate family member of the victim consents by way of a request to the court 3 and the court issues an order permitting the communication, then the defendant may 4 contact that person. 5 (b) That the defendant, if equipped with a global positioning monitoring 6 system, comply with the appropriate geographic restrictions, monitoring 7 conditions, and enforcement provisions of R.S. 15:571.36, 571. 37, and 571.38. 8 * * * PRESIDENT OF THE SENATE SPEAKER OF THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES GOVERNOR OF THE STATE OF LOUISIANA APPROVED:

Title: CRIMINAL PROCEDURE:  Provides for bail conditions for defendants charged with certain domestic offenses. (8/1/26)
Summary: , which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 94 Original 2026 Regular Session Wheat Present law requires that in determining a defendant's bail, the court consider the defendant's previous criminal history and whether the defendant poses a threat or danger to the victim if the offense is co
Content: The original instrument and the following digest, which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 94 Original 2026 Regular Session Wheat Present law requires that in determining a defendant's bail, the court consider the defendant's previous criminal history and whether the defendant poses a threat or danger to the victim if the offense is committed under the following: (1) Against the defendant's family or household member. (2) Against the defendant's dating partner. (3) Against one who is alleged to have committed the offense of domestic abuse battery. (4) Against one who is alleged to have committed the offense of battery of a dating partner. (5) Against one who is alleged to have committed the offense of stalking. (6) Against one who is alleged to have committed the offense of cyberstalking. (7) Against one who is alleged to have committed the offense of violation of protective orders. (8) Against one who is alleged to have committed the offense of unlawful communications. (9) Against one who is alleged to have committed a sexual assault. Present law authorizes the court to require as a condition of bail that the defendant refrain from going to the residence or household of the victim, the victim's school, and the victim's place of employment or otherwise contacting the victim in any manner whatsoever, and to refrain from having any further contact with the victim. Present law further authorizes the court to require as a condition of bail that the defendant be prohibited from communicating, by electronic communication, in writing, or orally, with a victim of the offense or with any of the victim's immediate family members. Authorizes certain communications if the victim or an immediate family member consents by way of a request to the court and the court issues an order permitting the communication. If an immediate family member of the victim consents by way of a request to the court and the court issues an order permitting the communication, then the defendant may contact that person. Proposed law adds provisions for determining bail for offenses alleged to include human trafficking and trafficking of children for sexual purposes. Proposed law provides that if the defendant is alleged to have committed the offense of human trafficking or trafficking of children for sexual purposes, the court, after considering previous criminal history and whether the defendant poses a threat or danger to the public, may prohibit the defendant from engaging in employment that involves any of the following: (1) A transportation network company driver. (2) Operation of any bus, taxicab, or limousine for hire. (3) A service worker who goes into a residence to provide any type of service. (4) Operation of any carnival or amusement ride. (5) A door-to-door solicitor, peddler, or itinerant vendor selling any type of goods or services including magazines or periodicals or subscriptions to magazines or periodicals. (6) Supervisory or disciplinary authority over children. (7) Operation or participation in the governance of any early learning center, residential home, or residence in which child care services are provided by a family child care provider or in-home provider. Effective August 1, 2026. (Amends C.Cr.P Art. 320(G))

Title: SCHOOLS:  Provides for approval for charter schools that specialize in vocational and technical education to meet workforce demands for high demand and high wage jobs. (8/1/26) (OR INCREASE GF EX See Note)
Summary: SCHOOLS:  Provides for approval for charter schools that specialize in vocational and technical education to meet workforce demands for high demand and high wage jobs. (8/1/26) (OR INCREASE GF EX See Note)
Content: Fiscal Note LEGISLATIVE FISCAL OFFICE Date: Dept./Agy.: Fiscal Note On: SB 70 95 SLS 26RS EDMONDS Author: Subject: Education Vocation and Technical Education Charter Schools March 23, 2026 10:11 AM ORIGINAL Bill Text Version: Opp. Chamb. Action: Proposed Amd.: Sub. Bill For.: Analyst: Julie Silva Proposed legislation is anticipated to result in an indeterminable, but expected to be minimal, increase in state expenditures, primarily related to administrative costs incurred by the Louisiana Department of Education (LDOE) for the third-party review of charter applications. Under present law (R.S. 17:3981), each charter application must be reviewed by a third party, who provides a recommendation to LDOE concerning approval. LDOE staff then make a final recommendation on approval to the Board of Elementary and Secondary Education (BESE). The increase in the number of schools that may apply as a result of this legislation is unknown, and actual expenditures are indeterminable. Given that there are currently only four VTE Type 2 charter schools operating with a corporate partner, the number of annual applications under the new pathway is expected to be minimal. For informational purposes, the estimated cost per component of the charter application third-party review process is provided below. Given a total standard application review cost of $19,100, five schools would need to be reviewed before costs reach a significant level. For applications processed under expedited review at a cost of $22,920, four applications annually would reach the significant expenditure threshold. Third-Party Review Unit Price Per Component Standard Expedited Review and Provision of Feedback on Initial Application $10,700 $12,840 Due Diligence Research $600 $720 Review and Provision of Feedback on Applicant 7-Page Response $1,500 $1,800 Capacity Interview and Performance Tasks $4,300 $5,160 Submission of Final Report and Local Analysis $2,000 $2,400 Total $19,100 $22,920 Minimal additional workload may result for the Louisiana Economic Development (LED) and the Louisiana Board of Commerce and Industry (LBCI) in issuing resolutions confirming qualifying economic sectors. This work is expected to be handled with existing personnel and resources. Note: LDOE reports that current staff are expected to absorb the workload of supporting BESE in the oversight of any new charters approved under proposed legislation, with an additional position required in the future only if a significant number of new schools are added. Proposed legislation expands present law to allow specialty vocational and technical education (VTE) charter schools to apply for a Type 2 charter without a corporate partner, contingent upon obtaining a resolution from the Louisiana Board of Commerce and Industry (LBCI) demonstrating a need for VTE in a designated economic sector. The bill also permits traditional public schools or charter schools to partner with VTE charter schools to meet current statutory requirements for offering vocational and technical education courses. To the extent students transfer from local school districts to state-authorized Type 2 charter schools, which act as their own local education agency, local school systems may experience a corresponding decrease in MFP funding. The overall revenue impact is expected to be neutral to minimal at the state level, with funding shifts occurring within the MFP formula based on student enrollment. Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 96 BY SENATORS EDMONDS, ABRAHAM, ALLAIN, BARROW, BASS, BOUDREAUX, CLOUD, CONNICK, FESI, HENSGENS, JENKINS, KLEINPETER, LAMBERT, MCMATH, MIGUEZ, MILLER, MORRIS, PRESSLY, PRICE, SEABAUGH, SELDERS, STINE AND WHEAT AND REPRESENTATIVES BOYER, SAWYER AND SCHAMERHORN 1 AN ACT 2 To enact R.S. 47:463.243, relative to motor vehicle special prestige license plates; to 3 establish the "Fallen Heroes" special prestige license plate; to provide for creation, 4 issuance, design, fees, distribution, and rule promulgation applicable to the license 5 plate; and to provide for related matters. 6 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 7 Section 1. R.S. 47:463.243 is hereby enacted to read as follows: 8 §463.243. Special prestige license plate; "Fallen Heroes" 9 A.(1) The secretary of the Department of Public Safety and Corrections 10 shall establish a special prestige motor vehicle license plate to be known as the 11 "Fallen Heroes" plate, provided there is a minimum of one thousand applicants 12 for the plates. The license plate shall be restricted to use on passenger cars, 13 pickup trucks, recreational vehicles, motorcycles, and vans. 14 (2) The Department of Public Safety and Corrections, office of motor 15 vehicles, shall create the special prestige license plate when the applicable 16 statutory provisions are met and the department's electronic vehicle and title 17 registration system is updated to accommodate the creation of new plates. 18 B. Provided the design is in compliance with R.S. 47:463(A)(3), the 19 design shall include the words "Fallen Heroes". 20 C. The special prestige license plate shall be issued, upon application, to 21 any citizen of Louisiana in the same manner as any other motor vehicle license 22 plate. 23 D. The department shall collect an annual royalty fee of twenty-five 24 dollars that shall be disbursed in accordance with Subsection E of this Section. 25 This fee shall be in addition to the standard motor vehicle license tax imposed ACT No. 206 SB NO. 96 ENROLLED 1 by Article VII, Section 5 of the Constitution of Louisiana, and a handling fee of 2 three dollars and fifty cents for each plate to be retained by the department to 3 offset a portion of administrative costs. 4 E. The annual royalty fee shall be collected by the department and 5 forwarded to Capital Area Law Enforcement Foundation for the purchase of 6 special protective equipment, commonly known as a bulletproof vest, for peace 7 officers in the state of Louisiana. 8 F. The secretary shall promulgate and adopt rules and regulations as are 9 necessary to implement the provisions of this Section. PRESIDENT OF THE SENATE SPEAKER OF THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES GOVERNOR OF THE STATE OF LOUISIANA APPROVED:

Title: AUTOMOBILE INSURANCE:  Provides for proof of insurance of transportation network companies involved in an accident. (8/1/26)
Summary: AUTOMOBILE INSURANCE:  Provides for proof of insurance of transportation network companies involved in an accident. (8/1/26)
Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 100 BY SENATOR JENKINS 1 AN ACT 2 To amend and reenact R.S. 45:201.9, relative to proof of insurance by transportation 3 network company drivers; to provide that a transportation network company driver 4 shall provide proof of insurance coverage in the event of an accident; to provide that 5 a transportation network company driver shall disclose whether he was logged on to 6 the transportation network company's digital network or on a prearranged ride at the 7 time of an accident; to provide for penalties; and to provide for related matters. 8 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 9 Section 1. R.S. 45:201.9 is hereby amended and reenacted to read as follows: 10 §201.9. Proof of insurance 11 A transportation network company driver shall carry written or digital proof 12 of coverage satisfying R.S. 45:201.6 with him at all times during his use of a vehicle 13 in connection with a transportation network company's digital network. In the event 14 of an accident, a transportation network company driver shall provide this insurance 15 coverage information to the directly interested parties, automobile insurers, and 16 investigating police officers, upon request pursuant to R.S. 32:863.1. Upon such 17 request, a transportation network company driver shall also disclose to directly 18 interested parties, automobile insurers, and investigating police officers, whether he 19 was logged on to the transportation network company's digital network or on a 20 prearranged ride at the time of an accident. Failure by a transportation network SB NO. 100 ENROLLED 1 company driver to comply with any provision of this Section shall subject the 2 transportation network driver to the provisions of R.S. 32:863.1. Title: PUBLIC LANDS:  Provides relative to surplus immovable property for affordable housing. (7/1/26)
Summary: , which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 301 Original 2026 Regular Session Duplessis Present law provides for the surplus of immovable property deemed nonessential by state agencies. Further requires the division of administration to prepare land use recommendations for the property. Pr
Content: The original instrument and the following digest, which constitutes no part of the legislative instrument, were prepared by Senate Legislative Services. The keyword, summary, and digest do not constitute part of the law or proof or indicia of legislative intent. [R.S. 1:13(B) and 24:177(E)] DIGEST SB 301 Original 2026 Regular Session Duplessis Present law provides for the surplus of immovable property deemed nonessential by state agencies. Further requires the division of administration to prepare land use recommendations for the property. Present law provides for the transfer of surplus property. Proposed law retains present law and establishes a process for transferring surplus immovable property to housing entities for the construction of affordable housing. Proposed law defines "affordable housing", "affordable housing entity", "deeply affordable housing", "very low-income resident", "low-income resident", "moderate-income resident", "permanent supportive housing", and "surplus immovable property". Proposed law requires the La. Housing Corp. to make an inventory of surplus immovable property on its website. Provides that the governor may determine that a property is essential to the state and requires that the governor provide written reasons for the determination. Proposed law provides that an award of property shall be made at least once a year. Proposed law provides that an affordable housing entity may submit a proposal to build affordable housing on a parcel and that proposals be evaluated by the board of the La. Housing Corp. based on inclusion of supportive housing, proportion of deeply affordable units, type of ownership, on-site disability services, and acceptance of tenant-based vouchers. Proposed law requires the La. Housing Corp. to notify the commissioner of administration of the number of properties transferred, number of housing units planned, and the type of developments its approves. Proposed law provides for the commissioner to oversee the cooperative endeavor agreement (CEAs) between the La. Housing Corp. and the affordable housing entity. Further allows the entity to exchange development services in exchange for property rights. Proposed law requires the La. Housing Corp. to provide a report to the legislature, governor, and commissioner providing an inventory of parcels transferred, total number of parcels conveyed, inventory of developments approved, and the total number of housing units planned and completed. Present law requires the division to provide a land management best use recommendation report to the legislative committees on natural resources and environment and any legislator in whose district a surplus immovable property is located. Prohibits the division from implementing its recommendation, unless approved by the legislative committees on natural resources and environment. Proposed law changes the report recipient to the La. Housing Corp and removes the requirement for legislative committee approval prior to division implementation of best use recommendations. Further requires the La. Housing Corp. to decide whether to retain the property for disposition pursuant to proposed law within 90 days of receipt of the division report. If the parcel is not retained for affordable housing development, proposed law requires the division to implement its recommendations. Present law requires the division submit an annual report to the legislative committees on natural resources and environment, providing the amount of property sold, agencies that held the property, costs, and proceeds from sales, and an updated inventory of property still owned by the state. Proposed law changes the report recipient to the legislature and the governor. Effective July 1, 2026. (Amends R.S. 41:140(B), (C), and (F); adds R.S. 40:600.112)

Title: COURTS:  Provides for the election sections in the First Judicial District Court and for one judge to be elected at large. (gov sig) (OR NO IMPACT LF EX See Note)
Summary: COURTS:  Provides for the election sections in the First Judicial District Court and for one judge to be elected at large. (gov sig) (OR NO IMPACT LF EX See Note)
Content: Fiscal Note OFFICE OF LEGISLATIVE AUDITOR Date: Dept./Agy.: Fiscal Note On: SB 267 302 SLS 26RS JENKINS Author: Subject: First Judicial District Court Election of Judges to the First Judicial District Court March 11, 2026 4:48 PM ORIGINAL Bill Text Version: Opp. Chamb. Action: Proposed Amd.: Sub. Bill For.: Analyst: Elle Craft Purpose of Bill: This bill provides for the restructuring of judicial elections within the First Judicial District Court, which solely encompasses Caddo Parish, by reallocating one judgeship seat (Division F) from Election Section Three to a district- wide at-large seat, resulting in four judges assigned to Election Section One, three judges to Election Section Two, three judges to Election Section Three, and one judge elected at large. This reallocation will take effect at the next regularly scheduled election following the vacancy of the Division F judgeship due to the currently serving judge’s death, resignation, retirement, incapacity, or decision not to seek re-election. The total number of judges for the First Judicial District Court remains unchanged at eleven. There is no anticipated direct material effect on governmental expenditures as a result of this measure. An official with the First Judicial District Court (Court) indicated that this bill is not expected to impact the Court's expenditures, as both the number of judges and court operations are expected to remain unchanged. An official with the Louisiana Secretary of State indicated that any costs associated with ballot changes for the new at-large seat are expected to be minimal. There is no anticipated direct material effect on governmental revenues as a result of this measure. Title: PROCUREMENT CODE:  Creates the "Interstate Technology Collaboration Act" to provide for use of cooperative purchasing for information technology system procurement. (gov sig) (EN SEE FISC NOTE GF EX See Note)
Summary: PROCUREMENT CODE:  Creates the "Interstate Technology Collaboration Act" to provide for use of cooperative purchasing for information technology system procurement. (gov sig) (EN SEE FISC NOTE GF EX See Note)
Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 303 BY SENATOR CLOUD 1 AN ACT 2 To amend and reenact R.S. 39:1702(A)(1) and to enact R.S. 39:1702(D), relative to the 3 Louisiana Procurement Code; to provide for cooperative purchasing for information 4 technology systems; to provide for an effective date; and to provide for related 5 matters. 6 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 7 Section 1. R.S. 39:1702(A)(1) is hereby amended and reenacted and R.S. 39:1702(D) 8 is hereby enacted to read as follows: 9 §1702. Cooperative purchasing authorized; participation in federal General Services 10 Administration vendor list 11 A.(1) Any public procurement unit may either participate in, sponsor, 12 conduct, or administer a cooperative purchasing agreement for the acquisition of any 13 supplies, services, personal, professional, consulting, and social services, major 14 repairs, or construction with one or more public procurement units or external 15 procurement activities or one or more private procurement units in accordance with 16 an agreement entered into between the participants. Such cooperative purchasing 17 may include but is not limited to joint or multi-party contracts between public 18 procurement units and open-ended state public procurement unit contracts which are SB NO. 303 ENROLLED 1 made available to local public procurement units. Procurement units within the 2 executive branch of state government which are subject to the authority of the 3 Office of Technology Services pursuant to R.S. 39:15.1 may also utilize this Part 4 for the procurement of information technology systems and information 5 technology services as provided in Part V-A of Chapter 1 of Subtitle I of this 6 Title. Nothing in this Part shall be construed to expand or limit the authority 7 of the Office of Technology Services as established in R.S. 39:15.1 through 15.3. 8 Such cooperative purchasing may include but is not limited to: 9 (a) Joint or multi-party contracts between public procurement units. 10 (b) Open-ended state public procurement unit contracts which are made 11 available to public procurement units. 12 (c) Contracts between a public procurement unit and an external 13 procurement activity under which the public procurement unit accesses and 14 utilizes an existing information technology system belonging to the external 15 procurement activity in exchange for payment of a share of the costs associated 16 with the system. The public procurement unit shall not be required to 17 participate in a competitive procurement process for such contracts, provided 18 that the external procurement activity originally acquired the system by means 19 of a contract procured through a competitive procurement process. 20 * * * 21 D. Procurement units within the executive branch of government which 22 are subject to the authority of the Office of Technology Services pursuant to 23 R.S. 39:15.1 may procure information technology systems and services as 24 provided in Part V-A of Chapter 1 of Subtitle I of this Title from the General 25 Services Administration supply schedules. Such purchases need not comply 26 with the competitive bidding requirements of this Chapter. 27 Section 2. This Act shall be known and may be cited as the "Interstate Technology 28 Collaboration Act". 29 Section 3. This Act shall become effective upon signature by the governor or, if not 30 signed by the governor, upon expiration of the time for bills to become law without signature SB NO. 303 ENROLLED 1 by the governor, as provided by Article III, Section 18 of the Constitution of Louisiana. If 2 vetoed by the governor and subsequently approved by the legislature, this Act shall become 3 effective on the day following such approval. PRESIDENT OF THE SENATE SPEAKER OF THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES GOVERNOR OF THE STATE OF LOUISIANA APPROVED:

Title: COLLEGES/UNIVERSITIES:  Provides for accreditation for public colleges and universities. (gov sig) (EN NO IMPACT See Note)
Summary: COLLEGES/UNIVERSITIES:  Provides for accreditation for public colleges and universities. (gov sig) (EN NO IMPACT See Note)
Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 304 BY SENATOR EDMONDS 1 AN ACT 2 To enact R.S. 17:3125.1 and Chapter 25-B of Title 17 of the Louisiana Revised Statutes of 3 1950, to be comprised of R.S. 17:3171 through 3176, relative to public post 4 secondary institutions and governing boards; to authorize the Board of Regents to set 5 policy regarding accreditation of public postsecondary institutions; to authorize 6 public postsecondary management boards to select an accreditor under certain 7 circumstances; to provide for coordination between the Board of Regents and public 8 postsecondary management boards; to provide for implementation; to provide for 9 electronic notification by certain institutions of higher education; to provide for 10 powers; to provide for duties; to provide for prohibitions; to provide for definitions; 11 to direct the Louisiana State Law Institute to make technical changes; to provide for 12 an effective date; and to provide for related matters. 13 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 14 Section 1. R.S. 17:3125.1 and Chapter 25-B of Title 17 of the Louisiana Revised 15 Statutes of 1950, comprised of R.S. 17:3171 through 3176, are hereby enacted to read as 16 follows: 17 §3125.1. Accreditation; powers of the Board of Regents 18 The Board of Regents shall have the power to set policy regarding the 19 accreditation of all public postsecondary institutions, as provided in Chapter 20 25-B of Title 17 of the Louisiana Revised Statutes of 1950. 21 * * * 22 CHAPTER 25-B. ACCREDITATION OF 23 PUBLIC POST SECONDARY INSTITUTIONS 24 §3171. Application 25 Except as otherwise specifically provided by law, this Chapter shall 26 govern the accreditation of all public postsecondary institutions. 27 §3172. Definitions SB NO. 304 ENROLLED 1 As used in this Chapter, the following terms have the following meanings 2 unless the context clearly indicates otherwise: 3 (1) "Board of Regents" means the coordinating body for public 4 postsecondary education that oversees policies, budgets, academic program 5 approval, and facility planning for all Louisiana universities, community 6 colleges, and technical schools, pursuant to Article VIII, Section 5 of the 7 Constitution of Louisiana and R.S. 17:3121. 8 (2) "Management board" means a governing board of a public 9 postsecondary education institution that is responsible for supervising the 10 day-to-day operations of such institutions under the overall jurisdictional 11 planning and budgetary authority of the Louisiana Board of Regents. Article 12 VIII of the Constitution of Louisiana provides that the management boards are 13 the Board of Supervisors of Louisiana State University and Agricultural and 14 Mechanical College, the Board of Supervisors for the University of Louisiana 15 System, the Board of Supervisors of Southern University and Agricultural and 16 Mechanical College, and the Board of Supervisors of Community and Technical 17 Colleges. 18 (3) "Public postsecondary institution" or "institution" means a public 19 college or university that is under the jurisdiction of a management board for 20 postsecondary education created by or under authority of the Constitution of 21 Louisiana. 22 §3173. Powers of the Board of Regents; coordination with management boards 23 A. In coordination with each management board of a public 24 postsecondary institution, the Board of Regents shall have the power to set 25 policy regarding each of the following: 26 (1) Accreditation of public postsecondary institutions. 27 (2) Specialized accreditation of any individual program or unit at a 28 public postsecondary institution where such accreditation is required. 29 B. The Board of Regents shall adopt policies to allow a public 30 postsecondary institution, subject to the approval of the institution's SB NO. 304 ENROLLED 1 management board, to seek a new institutional accreditor from a list of 2 accreditors recognized by the United States Department of Education or to seek 3 a new specialized accreditor from a list of specialized accreditors maintained by 4 the Board of Regents. 5 C. The Board of Regents, in coordination with the management boards, 6 shall develop a process of changing an institutional accreditor in a manner that 7 ensures that a public postsecondary institution remains accredited at all times 8 by an accreditor recognized by the United States Department of Education. 9 D. With respect to specialized accreditation of an individual program or 10 unit, the Board of Regents shall do all of the following: 11 (1) Identify a list of units, programs, and professional schools that 12 require specialized accreditation and a corresponding list of recognized 13 accreditors. 14 (2) Adopt policies, in coordination with the management boards, to 15 ensure that each program or unit requiring specialized accreditation remains 16 accredited at all times by an accreditor from the list of accreditors maintained 17 by the Board of Regents. 18 §3174. Process for accreditation; applications; accreditors 19 A. A public postsecondary institution may seek a new accreditor for the 20 institution or for a specialized program or unit so long as the selection of an 21 accreditor and the process of seeking accreditation complies with this Chapter. 22 B. If a public postsecondary institution seeks a new accreditor, the 23 institution shall apply for approval with the institution's management board. 24 C. The public postsecondary institution shall seek an accreditor that 25 prioritizes all of the following: 26 (1) Promotes credential success and workforce outcomes. 27 (2) Promotes educational quality and affordability. 28 (3) Promotes financial stability of the public postsecondary institutions. 29 (4) Ensures appropriate accountability through a rigorous annual review 30 of the faculty. SB NO. 304 ENROLLED 1 D. The management board may not approve an accreditor unless the 2 accreditor has agreed, in writing, that the accreditor will not condition the 3 initial accreditation and will not condition any continued accreditation upon 4 any violation of law, as prohibited in R.S. 17:3176. 5 §3175. Management boards of public postsecondary institutions 6 A. If a public postsecondary institution applies for a new accreditor, as 7 provided in R.S. 17:3174, the management board shall do all of the following: 8 (1) Review the institution's application and determine if the proposed 9 accreditor meets the standards and requirements set forth in this Chapter. 10 (2) Determine if the application for a new accreditor complies with the 11 policies set forth by the Board of Regents, as provided in R.S. 17:3173. 12 B. With respect to a public postsecondary institution's accreditor or any 13 application for a new accreditor, the management board shall, in coordination 14 with the Board of Regents, do all of the following: 15 (1) Ensure that each public postsecondary institution that is under the 16 management board's jurisdiction shall post on the institution's website the 17 status of its accreditation and other documentation that the management board 18 deems relevant that has been submitted to the accreditor to gain or maintain 19 accreditation. 20 (2) Specify the manner and frequency that the institution is to notify or 21 update the public on the progress of reaccreditation with the same accreditor 22 or the process of accreditation with a new accreditor through the institution's 23 website. 24 §3176. Prohibited accreditors 25 Notwithstanding any other provision of law to the contrary, no 26 accreditor may be chosen that conditions accreditation upon any violation of the 27 Constitution of Louisiana or any state law. 28 Section 2. The Louisiana State Law Institute, pursuant to its statutory authority, is 29 hereby directed to delete "Southern Association of Colleges and Schools Commission on 30 Colleges" and replace with "accreditor recognized by the United States Department of SB NO. 304 ENROLLED 1 Education" in R.S. 17:3164, 3223, 3351.17, and 5029 and in any other Section in Title 17 2 or in any other Section or Code of the Louisiana Revised Statutes of 1950. 3 Section 3. This Act shall become effective upon signature by the governor or, if not 4 signed by the governor, upon expiration of the time for bills to become law without signature 5 by the governor, as provided by Article III, Section 18 of the Constitution of Louisiana. If 6 vetoed by the governor and subsequently approved by the legislature, this Act shall become 7 effective on the day following such approval. Title: COLLEGES/UNIVERSITIES:  Requires the Board of Regents, Louisiana Works, and other state agencies to develop a publicly available statewide dashboard for career alignment that provides information related to educational and career opportunities. (8/1/26) (EN NO IMPACT See Note)
Summary: COLLEGES/UNIVERSITIES:  Requires the Board of Regents, Louisiana Works, and other state agencies to develop a publicly available statewide dashboard for career alignment that provides information related to educational and career opportunities. (8/1/26) (EN NO IMPACT See Note)
Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 305 BY SENATORS EDMONDS, BARROW, BOUDREAUX, CLOUD, HARRIS, HENRY, JENKINS, MORRIS, SEABAUGH, SELDERS AND WOMACK AND REPRESENTATIVES BROUSSARD, CARLSON, CARPENTER, CARVER, CHASSION, FREIBERG, TERRY LANDRY, MARTINEZ, ST. BLANC AND TAYLOR 1 AN ACT 2 To enact R.S. 17:3138.2 and 3138.13(C), relative to the Board of Regents; to provide for 3 Louisiana Works to collaborate with the Board of Regents to create a statewide 4 dashboard for career alignment; to provide for information on educational and career 5 opportunities; to provide for duties of the Board of Regents; to provide relative to the 6 collection and analysis of data on work-based learning; and to provide for related 7 matters. 8 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 9 Section 1. R.S. 17:3138.2 and 3138.13(C) are hereby enacted to read as follows: 10 §3138.2. Degree program and career alignment transparency; postsecondary 11 workforce outcomes dashboard 12 A. The Board of Regents, in collaboration with postsecondary education 13 management boards, Louisiana Works and other relevant state agencies, shall 14 develop, maintain, and make publicly available a statewide dashboard or series 15 of dashboards that provide information on the alignment between 16 postsecondary degree programs and workforce outcomes in Louisiana. 17 B. The dashboard or dashboards shall be designed to assist in the 18 following: 19 (1) Prospective and current students and their families in understanding 20 career outcomes associated with degree programs. 21 (2) Policymakers and state agencies in evaluating postsecondary 22 program alignment with workforce needs. 23 (3) Postsecondary institutions in strengthening program design and labor 24 market relevance. 25 C. To the extent permitted by state and federal law, the dashboard or ACT No. 99 SB NO. 305 ENROLLED 1 dashboards may include but shall not be limited to the following information, 2 disaggregated as appropriate: 3 (1) Degree program completion data. 4 (2) Employment outcomes following completion, including industry and 5 occupational placement. 6 (3) Median and range of wages at defined intervals following completion. 7 (4) Alignment of degree programs with high-demand or priority 8 occupations as identified by the state. 9 (5) Additional indicators determined by the Board of Regents to be 10 relevant to career alignment and economic development. 11 D. The Board of Regents shall ensure that the dashboard or dashboards 12 are updated on a regular basis, use consistent and clearly defined metrics, and 13 present information in a manner that is accessible and understandable to the 14 general public. 15 E. Nothing in this Section shall be construed to: 16 (1) Require the disclosure of personally identifiable information. 17 (2) Create performance funding requirements or mandates for 18 postsecondary institutions. 19 (3) Limit institutional autonomy over curriculum or academic 20 programming. 21 F. The Board of Regents may adopt rules and enter into data-sharing 22 agreements as necessary to implement this Section, subject to applicable state 23 and federal privacy laws. 24 * * * 25 §3138.13. Return on investment analysis; industry-based credentials 26 * * * 27 C. The Board of Regents and Louisiana Works shall develop procedures 28 to collect and analyze data on work-based learning through experiences such as 29 internships, cooperative education programs, apprenticeships, or other similar 30 forms of work-based learning. The data collected on work-based learning SB NO. 305 ENROLLED 1 experiences may include whether the experience is paid or unpaid, the duration 2 of the placement, and whether the placement is part of a registered or 3 credential-aligned program. Any procedures developed by the Board of Regents 4 and Louisiana Works shall ensure the collection and analysis of data necessary 5 to encourage and capture any work-based learning credits an individual may 6 have earned and the appropriate manner in which such credits may be 7 awarded. PRESIDENT OF THE SENATE SPEAKER OF THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES GOVERNOR OF THE STATE OF LOUISIANA APPROVED:

Title: RELIGIOUS FREEDOMS/LIBERTIES:  Creates the crime of obstruction of the freedom of worship in a church or other place of worship. (gov sig)
Summary: RELIGIOUS FREEDOMS/LIBERTIES:  Creates the crime of obstruction of the freedom of worship in a church or other place of worship. (gov sig)
Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 306 BY SENATORS EDMONDS, ALLAIN, BASS, CATHEY, CONNICK, FESI, FOIL, HENRY, HODGES, LAMBERT, MCMATH, MIGUEZ, MIZELL, MORRIS, MYERS, OWEN, PRESSLY, REESE, SEABAUGH, STINE, TALBOT, WHEAT AND WOMACK AND REPRESENTATIVES ADAMS, BOUDREAUX, BOYER, CHENEVERT, COX, HORTON AND WILEY 1 AN ACT 2 To enact R.S. 14:38.6 and Civil Code Art. 2315.14, relative to offenses affecting the free 3 exercise of religion; to create the crime of obstruction or interference with the 4 freedom of worship at a church or other place of worship; to provide for elements of 5 the offense; to provide for penalties; to provide for definitions; to provide for liability 6 for damages for offenses related to the obstruction of the freedom of worship in a 7 church or other place of worship; to provide for injunctive relief; to provide for 8 legislative intent; to provide for legislative purposes; to provide for an effective date; 9 and to provide for related matters. 10 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 11 Section 1. R.S. 14:38.6 is hereby enacted to read as follows: 12 §38.6. Obstruction of the freedom of worship in a church or other place of 13 worship 14 A. Obstruction of the freedom of worship at a church or other place of 15 worship is committed when a person does any of the following: 16 (1) Uses force, the threat of force, physical obstruction, intentional 17 injury, or attempted intentional injury, to intimidate or interfere with any 18 person lawfully exercising or seeking to exercise the right of the free exercise of 19 religion at a church or other place of worship that is protected by the First 20 Amendment to the Constitution of the United States of America and by Article 21 XII, Section 17 of the Constitution of Louisiana. 22 (2) Intentionally damages or destroys the property of a church or other 23 place of worship. SB NO. 306 ENROLLED 1 (3) Willfully denies an attendee, clergy, or member of the staff of a 2 church or other place of worship any of the following: 3 (a) Lawful freedom of movement on the property of the church or other 4 place of worship. 5 (b) Lawful use of any property or facilities of a church or other place of 6 worship. 7 (c) Lawful ingress and egress to and from the physical facilities of a 8 church or other place of worship. 9 (4) Willfully refuses or fails to leave the private property of the church 10 or other place of worship when requested to do so by the clergy, when the 11 person is committing, threatening to commit, or inciting others to commit any 12 disruptive activity that is intended to disrupt, impair, interfere with or obstruct 13 the worship service in a church or other place of worship. 14 (5) Engages in a disruptive activity at a church or other place of worship. 15 B. For purposes of this Section: 16 (1) "Attendee" means a person who is lawfully present in a church or 17 other place of worship, or is engaging in the lawful ingress and egress thereto. 18 (2) "Church or other place of worship" means any building, structure, 19 or defined outdoor area that is owned, leased, or otherwise lawfully possessed 20 by a religious organization that is used for organized religious worship or other 21 religious activities. 22 (3) "Clergy" means the pastor of the church, a deacon, or other member 23 of the religious organization who has authority over the administration of the 24 church or other place of worship or has been designated by the religious 25 organization with such authority. 26 (4)(a) "Disruptive activity" means intentional conduct occurring on the 27 property of a church or other place of worship during a worship service that 28 materially and substantially interferes with the service, including interruption 29 of the service or obstruction of access to any area used in connection with the 30 service, such as a sanctuary, chapel, nursery, children's ministry area, or other SB NO. 306 ENROLLED 1 designated worship-related space. 2 (b) "Disruptive activity" does not include behavior otherwise prohibited 3 by Subsection A of this Section. 4 (5) "Religious organization" means an entity that is operated for 5 religious purposes and qualifies for exemption from taxation under Section 6 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code of 1986 (Public Law 99-514, 26 U.S.C. 7 Section 501). 8 (6) "Worship service" means a regularly scheduled religious meeting or 9 assembly held at a church or other place of worship for the purpose of worship 10 or religious observance. 11 C.(1) Whoever commits obstruction of the freedom of worship in a 12 church or other place of worship shall be fined not more than two thousand five 13 hundred dollars, or imprisoned for not less than fifteen days nor more than six 14 months, or both. 15 (2) For a second or subsequent offense, the offender shall be fined not 16 more than twenty-five thousand dollars, or imprisoned with or without hard 17 labor for not less than one year nor more than eighteen months, or both. 18 (3) In addition to any other penalty imposed pursuant to this Subsection, 19 when the offense results in bodily injury of a lawful attendee, member of the 20 clergy, or staff member, the offender shall be sentenced to not less than two 21 years and not more than ten years at hard labor. 22 D. Any person who knowingly finances, funds, or otherwise provides 23 material support or assistance to another person with the intent to facilitate, 24 promote, or cause a violation of this Section shall be deemed to be a principal 25 pursuant to R.S. 14:24 and shall be subject to the same penalties as the person 26 who directly commits the violation of this Section. 27 Section 2. Civil Code Art. 2315.14 is hereby enacted to read as follows: 28 Art. 2315.14. Liability for damages caused by offenses related to obstruction of 29 freedom of worship in a church or other place of worship 30 A. In addition to general and special damages, a prevailing plaintiff shall SB NO. 306 ENROLLED 1 be awarded court costs and reasonable attorney fees upon proof that the 2 injuries on which the action is based were caused by conduct prohibited by R.S. 3 14:38.6 resulting in the injury to the person, obstruction of the freedom of 4 worship in a church or other place of worship, or damage to property, 5 regardless of whether the defendant was arrested, charged, or prosecuted for 6 such conduct. 7 B. Nothing in this Article shall be construed to limit any other cause of 8 action or remedy provided by law. 9 C. As used in this Article, a defendant includes any person who 10 knowingly aided, abetted, financed, funded, solicited, conspired, or otherwise 11 provided material support or assistance for conduct prohibited by R.S. 14:38.6. 12 D.(1) Recoverable damages pursuant to this Article may include but are 13 not limited to damages for physical injury, mental anguish, emotional distress, 14 property damage, lost income, interference with the free exercise of religion, 15 and any other damages authorized by law. 16 (2) In addition to any other remedy provided by law, a court may grant 17 temporary restraining orders, preliminary injunctions, permanent injunctions, 18 or declaratory relief, as well as court costs and reasonable attorney fees, to 19 prevent the conduct, or to prevent the continuation of the conduct, that is 20 prohibited by R.S. 14:38.6. 21 Section 3. The legislature finds and declares that: 22 (1) The free exercise of religion and the right of individuals to assemble for worship 23 services is protected by the First Amendment to the Constitution of the United States of 24 America and Article I, Section 8 of the Constitution of Louisiana. 25 (2) In 2023, the legislature and the people of Louisiana adopted Article XII, Section 26 17 of the Constitution of Louisiana which provides that "(t)he freedom to worship in a 27 church or other place of worship is a fundamental right that is worthy of the highest order 28 of protection". 29 (3) The intentional interference with a scheduled worship service undermines these 30 fundamental rights to the free exercise of religion and the freedom to worship in a church SB NO. 306 ENROLLED 1 or other place of worship by disrupting the orderly practice of religion and can create a 2 danger to persons attending the worship service. 3 (4) The right to engage in a peaceful protest is essential to a free society of people, 4 and that right extends to public places that are traditionally open to political speech and 5 debate, including public streets, sidewalks, and parks with minimal constitutional 6 restrictions; however, that right has never been interpreted to authorize the trespass or 7 disruptive activity on private property, such as a church or other place of worship or at a 8 home. 9 (5) Prevention of the willful disruption of worship services and religious activities 10 is a matter of statewide concern. 11 (6) It is a compelling interest of this state to protect the ability of its people to meet 12 and assemble for the exercise of religious worship and other religious activities guaranteed 13 by the Free Exercise Clause of the First Amendment to the Constitution of the United States 14 of America as well as Article I, Section 8 and Article XII, Section 17 of the Constitution of 15 Louisiana. 16 (7) It is imperative to a free society and the survival of a constitutional republic that 17 the state of Louisiana protects attendees in churches or other places of worship so that the 18 attendees are free from intentional acts that materially interfere or disrupt a scheduled 19 worship service or other religious activity that is conducted on the private property that is 20 set aside by the religious organization for constitutionally protected religious assemblies. 21 Section 4. The legislature declares that the purpose of this Act is to enact protections 22 that may be enforced at the state level, by state law enforcement authorities, in a similar 23 manner that federal law enforcement authorities, pursuant to 18 U.S.C. 248(a)(2), are able 24 to protect the free exercise of religion at a place of religious worship by prohibiting the use 25 of force, threats of force, or physical obstruction to injure, intimidate, or interfere with any 26 person exercising their right to worship at a church or other place of worship. 27 Section 5. If any provision or item of this Act, or the application thereof, is held 28 invalid, such invalidity shall not affect other provisions, items, or applications of the Act 29 which can be given effect without the invalid provision, item, or application and to this end 30 the provisions of this Act are hereby declared severable. SB NO. 306 ENROLLED 1 Section 6. This Act shall become effective upon signature by the governor or, if not 2 signed by the governor, upon expiration of the time for bills to become law without signature 3 by the governor, as provided by Article III, Section 18 of the Constitution of Louisiana. If 4 vetoed by the governor and subsequently approved by the legislature, this Act shall become 5 effective on the day following such approval. PRESIDENT OF THE SENATE SPEAKER OF THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES GOVERNOR OF THE STATE OF LOUISIANA APPROVED:

Title: PUBLIC BUILDINGS/GROUNDS:  Authorizes the educational display of the Ten Commandments as a historical document and monument. (8/1/26) (EN NO IMPACT See Note)
Summary: PUBLIC BUILDINGS/GROUNDS:  Authorizes the educational display of the Ten Commandments as a historical document and monument. (8/1/26) (EN NO IMPACT See Note)
Content: 2026 Regular Session ENROLLED SENATE BILL NO. 307 BY SENATORS EDMONDS AND HODGES AND REPRESENTATIVES ADAMS, AMEDEE, BACALA, BAMBURG, BAYHAM, BEAULLIEU, BILLINGS, BOUDREAUX, BOYER, BUTLER, WILFORD CARTER, CHENEVERT, COATES, CREWS, DESHOTEL, DEWITT, EGAN, FARNUM, FIRMENT, FONTENOT, HORTON, ILLG, MIKE JOHNSON, MACK, MCCORMICK, MOORE, OWEN, SAWYER, SCHAMERHORN, THOMAS, VENTRELLA AND WYBLE 1 AN ACT 2 To amend and reenact R.S. 25:1281, 1282(A), (B), and (C), and 1283 and to enact R.S. 3 49:150.1(J) and (K), relative to educational documents and monuments; to provide 4 with respect to the educational public displays of certain documents and monuments 5 related to the religious history of the nation and the state's culture, tradition, and 6 codes of law; to provide with respect to certain historical documents and monuments; 7 to authorize the Ten Commandments monument on the Louisiana State Capitol 8 grounds; to provide that the commissioner of administration shall select a proposal 9 and arrange for installation; to provide for design, construction, and installation of 10 the monument without cost to the state; to provide for content and limitations; to 11 authorize delegation of the commissioner's responsibilities; to provide for legislative 12 intent; and to provide for related matters. 13 Be it enacted by the Legislature of Louisiana: 14 Section 1. R.S. 25:1281, 1282(A), (B), and (C), and 1283 are hereby amended and 15 reenacted to read as follows: 16 CHAPTER 32. DISPLAY OF RELIGIOUS DOCUMENTS 17 AS CULTURE, TRADITION, AND THE HISTORY OF LAW 18 §1281. Legislative finding 19 The Legislature of Louisiana finds that: 20 (1) There is a need to educate and inform the public as to the history and 21 background tradition of American law and Louisiana law. 22 (2) America and Louisiana's religious history plays an important role in the 23 background of our American law and Louisiana law. 24 (3) The role of religion in the constitutional history of both the United States SB NO. 307 ENROLLED 1 of America and Louisiana is acknowledged by historians as part of our culture, 2 tradition, and history. 3 (4) The basic Basic knowledge of American legal history is important to the 4 formation of civic virtue in our society and, therefore, recognizing the historical 5 role of the Ten Commandments, the Mayflower Compact, the Declaration of 6 Independence, and the Northwest Ordinance of 1787 accords with the nation's 7 history and reflects the founders' understanding of the necessity of civic 8 morality to a functional self-government. 9 (5) The courts have provided vital direction to the Legislature of Louisiana 10 on how to approach the display of historical documents. 11 (6) In 2005, the Supreme Court of the United States recognized that it is 12 permissible to display the Ten Commandments on government property in Van 13 Orden v. Perry, 545 U.S. 677, 688 (2005). 14 (7) In 2019, the Supreme Court of the United States further recognized 15 that the Ten Commandments "have historical significance as one of the 16 foundations of our legal system. . .", in American Legion v. American Humanist 17 Association, 588 U.S. 29, 53 (2019) and, the court also ruled that the displaying 18 of the Ten Commandments on public property may have "multiple purposes" 19 such as "historical significance as one of the foundations of our legal system" 20 and represents a "common cultural heritage". Id. at 54. 21 (8) The Mayflower Compact of 1620 was America's first written 22 constitution and made a Covenant with Almighty God to "form a civil body 23 politic". This was the first purely American document of self-government and 24 affirmed the link between civil society and God. 25 (9) The Declaration of Independence, adopted by Congress on July 4, 26 1776, provides that "(we) hold these truths to be self-evident, that all men are 27 created equal, that they are endowed by their Creator with certain unalienable 28 Rights, that among these are Life, Liberty and the pursuit of Happiness". 29 (10) The Northwest Ordinance of 1787 provided a method of admitting 30 new states to the Union from the territory as the country expanded to the SB NO. 307 ENROLLED 1 Pacific. The Ordinance "extended the fundamental principles of civil and 2 religious liberty" to the territories and stated that "(r)eligion, morality, and 3 knowledge, being necessary to good government and the happiness of mankind, 4 schools and the means of education shall forever be encouraged". 5 (6)(11) The Legislature of Louisiana now endorses the prolongation of a 6 uniform, sound, distinct, and appropriate presentation of the story of the role of 7 religion in the constitutional history of the United States of America and Louisiana, 8 which may be publicly displayed in court houses, and other state and local buildings, 9 at the Louisiana State Capitol, and throughout the state of Louisiana. 10 (12) The text of the Ten Commandments set forth in R.S. 25:1282(C) is 11 identical to the text of the Ten Commandments monument that was upheld by 12 the Supreme Court of the United States in Van Orden v. Perry, 545 U.S. 677, 688 13 (2005). 14 (13) It is the legislature's intent to apply the decision set forth by the 15 Supreme Court of the United States in Van Orden v. Perry, id. to continue the 16 rich tradition and ensure that the public has an opportunity to understand and 17 appreciate the foundational documents of our state and national government. 18 (14) The Supreme Court of the United States acknowledged that the Ten 19 Commandments may be displayed on government property when a private 20 donation is made for the purchase of the historical monument. Pleasant Grove 21 City v. Summum, 555 U.S. 460 (2009). 22 (15) It is the intention of the Legislature of Louisiana that this Section 23 shall not create an unfunded mandate on any public governing authority. 24 Government agencies are encouraged to use documents that are printed, or 25 displays that are produced, and made available to the agency at no cost. 26 §1282. Public displays of religious history impacting the law 27 A. Public displays with acknowledged religious history may include, but shall 28 not be limited to, any of the items in this Section. 29 B. The Mayflower Compact, written and adopted in 1620, the text of which 30 reads as follows: SB NO. 307 ENROLLED 1 Mayflower Compact (1620 A.D.) 2 Agreement Between the Settlers at New Plymouth: 1620 3 IN THE NAME OF GOD, AMEN. We, whose names are underwritten, the 4 Loyal Subjects of our dread Sovereign Lord, King James, by the Grace of God, of 5 Great Britain, France, and Ireland, King, Defender of the Faith. Having undertaken 6 for the Glory of God, and Advancement of the Christian Faith, and the Honour of our 7 King and Country, a voyage to plant the first colony in the northern Parts of Virginia; 8 do by these presents, solemnly and mutually, in the Presence of God and one 9 another, covenant and combine ourselves together into a civil Body Politick, for our 10 better Ordering and Preservation, and Furtherance of the Ends aforesaid: And by 11 Virtue hereof do enact, constitute, and frame, such just and equal Laws, Ordinances, 12 Acts, Constitutions and Offices, from time to time, as shall be thought most meet and 13 convenient for the General good of the Colony; unto which we promise all due 14 submission and obedience. In Witness whereof we have hereunto subscribed our 15 names at Cape Cod the eleventh of November, in the Reign of our Sovereign Lord 16 King James, of England, France, and Ireland, the eighteenth, and of Scotland the 17 fifty-fourth, Anno Domini; 1620. 18 C. The Ten Commandments, as extracted from the Bible. The text shall read 19 as follows: 20 "The Ten Commandments 21 I AM the LORD thy God. 22 Thou shalt have no other gods before me. 23 Thou shalt not make to thyself any graven images. 24 Thou shalt not take the Name of the Lord thy God in vain. 25 Remember the Sabbath day, to keep it holy. 26 Honor thy father and thy mother, that thy days may be long upon the land which 27 the Lord thy God giveth thee. 28 Thou shalt not kill. 29 Thou shalt not commit adultery. 30 Thou shalt not steal. SB NO. 307 ENROLLED 1 Thou shalt not bear false witness against thy neighbor. 2 Thou shalt not covet thy neighbor's house. 3 Thou shalt not covet thy neighbor's wife, nor his manservant, nor his maidservant, 4 nor his cattle, nor anything that is thy neighbor's." 5 * * * 6 §1283. Context of public display 7 Public displays set forth in R.S. 25:1282(B), (C), (D), and (E) shall be 8 accompanied by a document entitled "Context for Acknowledging America's 9 Religious History as Culture, Tradition, and the History of Law" which shall read 10 as follows: 11 (1) Some documents stand out as pivotal in the religious history of America 12 and Louisiana's legal system, among which are the Mayflower Compact, The 13 Declaration of Independence as a legal foundation for the United States Constitution, 14 the Ten Commandments as one of the foundations of our legal system, and the 15 Northwest Ordinance, which was a primary document affirming faith and the first 16 congressional act legally prohibiting slavery. It is hoped that their study and relation 17 to each other and the history of our state and nation will foster an appreciation for 18 the role that religion has played in the culture, tradition, and legal history of the 19 law of America and the state of Louisiana and prompt further public study. 20 (2) American law, constitutionalism, and political theory have deep roots in 21 religion. American ideals about liberty, freedom, equality, legal responsibility and 22 codes of law, to mention a few, have roots and underpinnings in religion and biblical 23 literacy. The Ten Commandments, which are found in the Book of Exodus in the Old 24 Testament of the Bible, was were one of the earliest written expressions of law to be 25 incorporated in American legal systems. The Ten Commandments, or the law of 26 nature, also impacted the Declaration of Independence which refers to the "laws of 27 nature and of Nature's God." 28 Section 2. R.S. 49:150.1(J) and (K) are hereby enacted to read as follows: 29 §150.1. State capitol complex; allocation of space; maintenance; law enforcement 30 officer and firefighter memorial; Gold Star Families monument; SB NO. 307 ENROLLED 1 African-American military service members monument; the Ten 2 Commandments 3 * * * 4 J.(1) The commissioner of administration shall permit and arrange for 5 the placement on the Louisiana State Capitol grounds of a suitable monument 6 displaying the Ten Commandments. The commissioner shall designate the 7 location of the monument, which shall be among other monuments, and shall 8 oversee its installation. The design, construction, placement, and maintenance 9 of the monument shall be free of charge to the state. The commissioner may 10 delegate his responsibilities provided in this Subsection to the office of state 11 buildings. 12 (2) The commissioner shall accept and consider proposals for the 13 monument from private entities and shall select the proposal for the monument 14 that will be installed. In selecting a proposal, the commissioner shall consider 15 the appropriateness of the proposed design, the demonstrated ability of the 16 responsible entity to pay the cost of design, construction, installation, and 17 maintenance of the monument, and any other factor he deems appropriate. The 18 commissioner shall make such selection by December 31, 2026; however, if he 19 has not received an acceptable proposal by that date, he shall select the first 20 acceptable proposal he receives after that date. 21 (3)(a) The overall dimensions of the monument shall not exceed six feet 22 in height, four feet in width, and four feet in depth, including any base on which 23 the monument sits. 24 (b) The monument shall use the same text provided in R.S. 25:1282, 25 which is the same text that was used in Van Orden v. Perry, 545 U.S. 677 (2005), 26 that was ruled constitutional by the Supreme Court of the United States. 27 (c) The monument shall include a plaque that includes the text of the 28 "Context for Acknowledging America's Religious History as Culture, Tradition, 29 and the History of Law" as set forth in R.S. 25:1283. 30 K.(1) An outdoor display or monument authorized pursuant to this SB NO. 307 ENROLLED 1 Section shall be maintained in a manner that preserves the structural integrity, 2 legibility, and respectful appearance of the display or monument. 3 (2) The commissioner shall ensure that each outdoor display or 4 monument is maintained in accordance with this Subsection, including 5 providing for any necessary cleaning, repair, or restoration resulting from 6 weathering, vandalism, or other damage. Responsibility for such maintenance 7 shall be assigned to the private entity selected to provide the display or 8 monument or to another private entity selected by the commissioner. 9 (3) If the entity responsible for maintenance fails to maintain the display 10 or monument in accordance with this Subsection, the commissioner of 11 administration shall provide written notice of the deficiency and a reasonable 12 opportunity to cure the deficiency. If the deficiency is not timely cured and the 13 commissioner is unable to secure another private entity to assume responsibility 14 for maintenance, the commissioner may remove the display or monument.
